qa_pairs.question: When submitting a job to the SLURM scheduler on Trillium, how are resource requests and other job parameters specified within the job script, and what happens if memory is requested?
qa_pairs.answer: Resource requests and job parameters are specified using `#SBATCH` directives, which are placed in separate comment lines at the top of the job script, after `#!/bin/bash`. Common options include `--nodes`, `--ntasks-per-node`, `--time`, `--job-name`, `--output`, `--mail-type`, `--gpus-per-node`, `--partition`, and `--account`. It is important to note that any memory requests made using the `--mem` option are ignored on Trillium; users are automatically allocated all available memory on their assigned nodes or GPUs.

qa_pairs.question: What are the available job partitions and their typical limits for CPU compute jobs on the Trillium cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: For CPU compute jobs on Trillium, there are two primary partitions: `compute` and `debug`. The `compute` partition is the default and allows up to 150 running jobs and 500 submitted jobs, with a minimum job size of 1 node (192 cores) and a maximum walltime of 24 hours. The maximum job size for the `compute` partition is 10 nodes (1920 cores) by default, or 128 nodes (24576 cores) for users with a Resource Allocation Project (RAC) allocation. The `debug` partition is for testing and troubleshooting, allowing 1 running and 1 submitted job, with a minimum size of 1 node (192 cores) and a maximum walltime of 1 hour.
